string.text: {=6NorKoWz}Well, before we begin — here's a flagon of ale to your health, both of the body and the soul.

{=ruX5vDpE}I fancy myself a philosopher of medicine, you see. My parents sent me to a monastery when I was still a boy, to study both the healing power of the Heavens and the remedies of the earth — the science of herbs, that is to say.

{=JrnXaI9i}But the monasteries, you know... The gardens are lovely, the libraries rich. But waking at dawn to pray for the health of the king and his patrons — well, I don't know if it helped his Majesty much, but it certainly wasn’t doing *me* any good. So I left.

{=vhQVhYFV}I began following the armies. There’s always work for a herbalist. I don’t care much for dawn marches, but they’re rare enough — and there’s often a spare cart to sleep on. As for warriors, they suffer wounds of the body and of the soul. I do far more good among them than I ever did in a monastery, I can tell you that.
{=hGYQ0mxR}Yes, yes. A healer must go where the wounded are.
{=astXdbzl}The monastic life is meant to raise us to purity. It does not look well when one turns away from it.
{=ataDrzt7}In any case, I’m looking for a campaign worth marching in.

{=BFVlgN2S}I was a monk once, but like many, I wasn’t well suited to that life, and was eventually asked to leave. I hold no grudge. The one gift the monastery gave me was knowledge of herbs and healing. And that, at least, has earned me my bread.
{=U0zuHtwa}Of course – and I'm sure you'll agree it's a tale worthy of the telling!

{=ELXn5Nv5}My lineage is as ancient as it is noble. When Prince Het'um summoned his lords for campaign, I took my father's horse and mail and rode out with pride. This was in our struggle against the Turkic raiders who had been harrying our borders.

{=nI3aJA8u}There was much marching and waiting, and I shan't bore you with all that. But when we sighted the enemy and began forming for battle, one lordling – if he deserves the title – told me to join the second rank. Me! As though I were some hireling without blood or name.

{=qMPY1rUo}Well, you know what a man of honor does when he is insulted – I struck him down, and when his kin came to defend him, I struck them down as well! I laid a full dozen of them low before they overbore me. Have you ever heard of a nobler stand against such odds?
{=OgdD6bs9}If you truly bested a dozen men alone, that's quite impressive.
{=26RU4ZRj}Wait... This famous stand was against your own side?

{=FqW4dBTS}The battle was lost, and the field scattered. I have no wish to return to a camp of cowards and backstabbers. But if another captain would have me, I would gladly add my sword – and my honor – to his cause.
{=kSJEjvHm}I am a knight of noble blood. I am a man of honor. Honor, of course, demands one avenge every insult – whatever the cost. That’s why I’m looking for new command. Let us leave it at that.
{=D7IINWEl}I can tell you my story, but I don't know how interesting you will find it.
{=QlWhygXi}I was born out of wedlock. My father took me in, but of course there was no question of me inheriting anything. I did not wish to be a burden on my brothers, so I decided to go to war.

{=2VTD9b4L}As it happened, my neighbors needed to supply an armed man for the king's levy. None of them wished to go, so I offered to stand in for them. They bought me a sword, jerkin and horse, and thus I became a warrior.

{=8xrIamsU}My first foray into war, we were pursued by a force of Mamluk cavalry twice our number. One man was needed to stay behind and hold a ford, so that the rest could escape. The others had families, so I chose to be that one, as I could think of none who would mourn me.
{=faDAEda5}I commend your love for your fellow man.
{=9YDjFZIU}Mmm... I do appreciate virtue, but villainy makes for a much spicier tale.

{=4mgqRx6a}I fought as best I could, but the Mamluk forces took me prisoner. When they found that there was no one to ransom me, they let me go on my pledge that I would pay them later. It was a most noble gesture of them. So, I feel that I have no choice but to earn the money.

{=qE4rBOI7}I am the son of a knight, but not a son born in wedlock. I was raised in my father's house, but when he was taken prisoner and ransomed, he had to sell much of what he owned, and therefore could no longer support me. I decided to set out to make my own name, if my family could not give me one. It is a common tale.
{=D6Xd5ZrT}I'm drunk, so I'll tell you.

{=xl6SnA6n}I was a soldier, like my father before me. We served under a nakharar lord in the Taurus mountains. I thought myself ready — not afraid of death or hunger. But war teaches lessons no monastery scroll can prepare you for.
{=xSKbicft}There was one man — a brother to me. He once took a blow meant for my neck. Said nothing about it, just smiled. The kind of comrade you’d follow into the gates of Hell itself.

{=4MJsYhys}But in the sack of Tarsus, I saw another side. He cut down merchants who’d surrendered. Took a boy’s ring from his mouth. I said nothing at first. But when our sparapet considered hanging him to placate the Catholicos, he asked for a witness. I could have lied. I didn’t.
{=g1sQna0D}A terrible dilemma.
{=nmtbhSbc}A warrior stands beside his comrades like a man stands beside his brothers. Whatever their crimes may be.
{=spHoDrGN}He hanged. I thought I did right, but my brothers looked at me differently. I couldn't bear their silence. So I left. I've been wandering ever since.
{=bxKXHPp1}I fought for one of the princes of Cilicia. Once I was proud to wear my sash. Now I carry only my blade and my regrets. Maybe there's still some honor left to be won.
{=q977EaUU}You ask a lot of questions, friend. So I'll tell you what you need to know, and no more.

{=I9FWCRtO}You look like someone who's been around a bit, so I probably don't have to tell you about how this faithless world of ours works. Sometimes it needs a man who's not afraid to get his hands dirty to make sure there's a bit of order.

{=5Z9SNzQw}The lords and the merchants, they sit in their lofty towers making grand decisions, but they don't see into the back alleys to know what plots are going on. They don't know where to find the debtor who won't pay his debt. They don't know where to find the wagging tongues starting rumors. So that's where I come in.
{=aTGl3b1G}Anyway, that's all I'll say about my line of work - unless, of course, you'd care to make a down payment on my services. Then we can talk more.
{=RsE01mvh}Ah, yes, you do sound like a useful type.
{=bDidH1Pu}It takes a vile man to do that work, and an even worse one to boast about it.
{=dJxHX9si}The way I look at it, we all fulfil the will of the Heavens in our own way. So I do what I'm good at.

{=ohA8pdlS}I kill for a living. I've killed a lot, and if you want me to kill for you, you'll hire me. You seem to have been around a bit. I don't think I need to tell you any more. I'm sure you can reckon if I'm worth my price.
{=ZN3QD4Ks}I'll tell you. I'll tell anyone who listens.

{=8Caa7QPs}My father had five sons. That wasn't unusual, but what was unusual is that we all made it to adulthood. There wasn't enough land for all of us, so the two youngest of us moved to Ayas. Found work on the wharves, hauling in cargos from all the ships coming in. For a few years, it was good work.

{=fGhNWKgK}But with the wars, and a lot of people losing their land, things changed on the docks. Too many hands, and not enough cargos. Gangs from the back alleys started getting involved. If you didn't hire their people, maybe your ship would go up in flames.

{=ddQAXhd6}One night I was walking home with my brother when we ran into a group working for one boss they called Big Tuna. Told us our crew had killed one of theirs, and they were going to kill one of ours in return. Took my brother, wrapped a heavy chain around his legs, and threw him into the water. I don't know why they let me live.
{=JsP90A6a}I am sorry for your loss.
{=9KJdwiWV}Aye, well, that shows how far honest work gets you.

{=FbVJrA77}So I ran. I left Ayas. Look, I know about shipping, cargos, inventories. I'm not a violent man. But I will learn to fight. And then I'll return. I'll show them that there's a price to be paid for killing my brother.

{=KAK7DLTq}My family were farmers, but we were driven off of our land. We tried to make a new home in Ayas, but the people weren't too keen on newcomers, and one of the gangs ran us out. Now I don't know what to do to myself, but whatever it is, I reckon I'll have to learn how to fight.
{=UnNwWJT2}I don't mind telling.

{=W2x5Z40R}In the hills of the north, near the passes to Lesser Armenia, we sleep with our spears nearby. You never know if one night it’ll be brigands, mercenaries, or a rival clan coming down. We’re proud folk up there — too proud for our own good, maybe.

{=fGTeaCVU}My father had more pride than most. One day, he quarreled with the steward of a local lord and swore he’d bow to no man again. He took us up into the high forests where no tithes were taken, no orders given.

{=q3ZXATdq}We burned plots, sowed barley, hunted, buried my mother when a fever took her — and lived that way near a decade. One winter, my father walked into the snow and never returned. I dug the earth that spring with fists like stones.
{=WZyybR2g}A tragic tale. My condolences.
{=f4kz9PyG}A fitting reward for a man who defied the order that the Heavens instituted on earth for our own good.

{=b0zmbTPQ}We came down from the heights, my sister and I. She married into a village. I found I had a knack for tracking — not much good for farming, but I can follow a trail by torchlight. So now I follow the road.

{=sfgZhUgT}I was raised far up in the Cilician highlands, beyond the reach of lords or taxes. A hard, cold life — not fit for a young man forever. I’m no courtier, but I can follow tracks and live off the land. If you need a scout, I’m your man.
{=6KUJ7cYt}Sure, I'll tell you my tale. Don't expect a happy one.

{=iPCX96mA}My father was a tenant farmer near Sis. By royal decree, if he paid his share of the crop, he could not be evicted. But our landlord wanted the land cleared for sheep, so he forged a deed claiming our family had no legal rights. And that was the end of us.

{=xB6zj8ei}I wasn’t about to beg. My cousin had lost his land before and joined a band of rebels who called themselves the *Sons of the Mountain*. They claimed to fight for the poor, to take back from the unjust. So I followed him.

{=3AbNYbOo}Maybe they believed that once. But when I joined, they were just extortionists. They stole grain, beat peasants who didn’t give enough, and called any dissent ‘betrayal.’ I called them what they were — bandits. They tried to kill me. My cousin shielded me and died for it.
{=1paC3mV1}Yes. Too many men on both sides of the law prey on the weak.
{=VLIz9b7u}You thought there was such a thing as noble bandits? Perhaps you went in search of angels next?

{=b2UpIO43}I left the mountains, but I learned woodcraft and how to move unseen. Now I hunt the very scum I once followed. I don’t claim to be noble — I just take the coin. But there’s a landlord out there who’s still breathing. One day, I’ll fix that.

{=WtNQGw3o}My family were tenants. The land was stolen, and I ran to the hills with rebels. Rebels? Hah. They were just glorified brigands. Now I’m back among men. Still looking for a cause that isn’t garbage.
{=JFdjrbAF}Huh? Well, okay. Why not?

{=1Gr3wo1m}I was a smith. You can see I'm a big man, strong... I was good at my trade. I had a workshop near the church square in Ayas. I’d saved some coin and was thinking about finding a woman to marry.

{=jsuOEu3a}There was a woman who’d fled the fighting near the border. She had a baby — her only family. She used to make wheat cakes over a brazier, sell them to pilgrims. One day a young noble from the Catholicos’ court came galloping through the street, drunk, and his horse crushed the child without even slowing.

{=rpOla1Vp}I’d seen that woman beg for flour, seen her keep that child alive through sickness and winter. And to watch it end like that... He shouted at her, calling her a beggar. I didn’t think. I crossed the square, raised my hammer, and ended him right there. It was the end of my life in Ayas, but I don’t regret it.
{=bwthhDSN}Right or wrong, I cannot blame you for your anger.
{=hpKAp2ZW}The law is the law, and murder is murder.

{=DHCTLcp1}So now I wander. I’ve worked guarding caravans and hauling grain. So long as no one asks me to hurt the helpless, I’ll keep swinging my hammer. Someday, if the Heavens allow, I’ll open a forge again.

{=XEsq2Gd6}I was a smith in Ayas, but a noble’s son wronged me and I struck him down. I had to flee. A forge doesn’t travel well, but strength and skill with a hammer do. So now I work for coin until I find peace again.
{=LTvInwfa}I have a cup of wine before me. The room is warm. Why not?

{=P1fLl2KS}Some girls fall for troubadours. My mother? She liked men with knives and stolen saddlebags. One day, a band came through her village near Hadjin, and she followed them. Said she married the leader. He got killed in a raid, so she married his second. Then his killer. And so on.

{=PIu7b9wO}I was born somewhere around husband four. My real father was hanged by the lords' guards before I could speak. Two others were stabbed in their sleep. You see, to lead bandits, you have to be the kind of man who never backs down — and doesn't plan to grow old. Most don’t.

{=LVkWjJgD}I grew up sleeping under trees and stealing from granaries. But after one brutal winter, my mother died of fever. I was twelve. I walked to Sis and told the gate guard I could use a blade. Been working odd jobs since then. Mercenary, caravan guard, tavern muscle. Not a bad life. At least the wine’s warm and the blankets don’t have lice.
{=JJqwFLjR}That's a start. Have you ever considered something honest?
{=587q6IS6}Charming. You sound like someone who enjoys a last meal as much as the first.
{=aOSiXn2O}If the pay's good and the boss isn’t a fool, I’ll stick around. Banditry’s for the starving. I’d rather eat anchovy stew with bread and drink the strong stuff they brew in Tarsus.

{=SmWz2t65}My mother followed a bandit into the woods. I followed her, grew up with knives and smoke and not much else. I’ve traded that life for one with fewer fleas, and better wine. But I still know how to swing a blade.
{=bE3LXxhw}Yeah... I can tell you all that.

{=PGa4Ehaa}My father was a wheelwright. He’d heard that the best work was down in Antioch, where they repaired the ox-carts of lords and traders. So we left Sis, sold our shop, and moved. The rumors were true — for a while. Then came the riots. Fires swept through the merchant quarter, and my father was killed in the street, just for speaking Armenian.

{=21QinZCq}The fighting never really stopped after that. With the wars, few care for broken wagons — but siege engines? They always need repairs. I got work near the fortress, and it turns out making a proper torsion twist wasn’t so different from setting a wagon axle. So I learned. I got good, too.

{=b3IzpFH2}Now I'm looking for work with a proper captain — someone who might need a catapult raised, or a gate smashed. And if one day I find myself behind a mangonel, lobbing rocks into Antioch... well, I won’t weep.
{=W5OECu87}A good engineer is always in demand.
{=YgZJYYds}Few war leaders have the patience for followers with private vendettas.
{=IX7IpWAm}Anyway, if you hear of anyone with a siege line and coin to spare, you send them my way.

{=qFLZGWHj}I was a wheelwright’s daughter, but these days there’s more coin in engines of war than in wagons. I’ve patched up mangonels and loaded trebuchets. Might be I could build one, too, if I had the time and tools.
{=anGYtcYU}Yeah... Right, so here it is...

{=kBFbivPP}I fought with the Red Company, a band of mercenaries that served whoever paid in silver. Some of them were good lads. Others... well, that kind of life draws wolves, and not all of them howl before they bite.

{=U0tO8xtz}There was one fellow, a Frank named Alric. I couldn’t say what made him hate me, but he did. Always looking at me cross, whispering, stirring up trouble. I learned to sleep with a dagger in hand.

{=u6mV420C}Before one siege, he told me plain he'd put a bolt in my back if I climbed ahead of him. But he went up the ladder first, so I did what I had to do. Put the bolt in him instead. He fell back, skull cracked on the rocks. That was the end of it.
{=QrFrfN44}I suppose that is self-defense, in a way.
{=xOSV0qT8}Shooting a man in the back is murder, whatever the circumstances.
{=l8Xo44xM}I left the company that day. His drinking mates knew what happened, and I wasn't about to wait for payback. Now I fight for coin, but only if the enemy's in front of me. That’s all I ask.
{=7RyuT39x}I served with a mercenary company for a time. Not the most loyal of brothers, but we got the job done. These days, I prefer simpler arrangements. I fight, you pay.
{=cvGrEGoT}Ah... I suppose I can tell you.

{=5xVPlyeI}I was a gamekeeper near Sis, in service to a noble whose name I won't speak. I was taught to track deer and boar through the scrublands and hills. But my master came to prefer another sort of prey.

{=PTc6N9tC}He ordered me to find a band called the Hill Brethren — outlaws, yes, but known as much for their hunger as their blades. I followed their sign through the cedar woods and found their camp. Knights rode in. They surrendered without a fight.

{=3ArVOTld}I thought they'd be brought to trial, perhaps a few hanged. Instead, they strung all twenty up like game outside the walls. Some were barely more than boys. Their faces haunted me in every dream. I'd tracked them. Their deaths were my work. And that... was not what I signed up for.
{=QSogTAKq}That sounds terrible indeed.
{=hxPlbuMf}These are hard times. Don't worry about the fate of outlaws.

{=IIaMv3C5}Now I offer my bow and my eyes to those who will use them with some measure of honor. War kills, yes — but cruelty is a choice. If I follow you, that is all I ask: kill like a hunter, not like a butcher.
{=sr5qg0s1}I'm a gamekeeper by trade, a tracker. I’ve served lords, but none whose orders I could respect. Maybe you’ll be different. Or maybe not. I’ll see.
